title: Collusion in Brokered Markets
abstract: High commissions in the U.S. residential real estate agency market pose a puzzle for economic theory because brokerage is not a concentrated industry. We model brokered markets as a game in which agents post prices for customers and then choose which other agents to work with. We show that there exists an equilibrium in which each agent conditions working with other agents on those agents' posted prices. Prices can therefore be meaningfully higher than the competitive level (for a fixed discount factor), regardless of the number of agents. Thus, brokered markets can remain uncompetitive even with low concentration and easy entry.

article.introduction:
The real estate industry inthe United States is characterized by widespread price coordination: The brokerage fee is typically 6%, with half of the fee going to the buyer's agent and half going to the seller's agent. There is little evidence that the 6% fee represents the true cost of facilitating a real estate transaction as it has been constant for decades (despite substantial changes in the technology used), varies with neither market conditions nor the price of the house being sold, and is appreciably higher than in many other countries (Delcoure and Miller (2002)).1Accordingly, the question of whether the price of real estate agency is artificially high due to collusion has concerned scholars since at least 1977 (Owen (1977)) and policymakers since at least 1983 (Federal Trade Commission (1983)). In recent years, a number of lawsuits have argued that realtors conspire to keep commissions high. The first of these cases,Sitzer v. NAR et al.(U.S. Dist. Ct. W.D. Mo., Case No. 4:19-cv-00332), was decided in October 2023, with a jury finding that the National Association of Realtors (NAR) and real estate agency franchisors such as Keller Williams had indeed engaged in anticompetitive behavior.2Yet, the market for providing residential real estate brokerage services is both highly unconcentrated and easy to enter (Beck, Scott, and Yelowitz (2012)), in which case price competition should be expected to drive brokerage fees down.3Thus, as Hsieh and Moretti (2003) observe in their influential study of real estate brokerage in the United States, “the apparent uniformity of commission rates presents an enormous puzzle.”4
In addition to real estate agency, a number of other important two-sided markets feature both independent buyer and seller representation and high prices. For instance, spreads are notoriously high in the primary market for municipal bonds in the United States (Green, Hollifield, and Schürhoff (2007b), Cestau et al. (2019)); underwriters represent municipalities wishing to issue municipal bonds while financial planners and advisors represent high-income individuals wishing to purchase such bonds (Green, Hollifield, and Schürhoff (2007a)). Similarly, both buyers and sellers of stocks listed on the Nasdaq generally trade through dealers, who “protected” their spreads (i.e., profits) from so-called “SOES [small order execution system] bandits” by avoiding quoting prices that used odd-eighths of a dollar (Durr and Colby (2010)) and by avoiding transactions with dealers who did quote odd-eighths. This behavior, first documented by Christie and Schultz (1994), is analogous to the in-period punishments that are key to supporting collusion in our framework.5
In this paper, we provide a potential explanation for how brokerage industries can maintain high prices even in the presence of many independent brokers, focusing on real estate agency as our lead application.6We model the market for brokerage services as a repeated extensive-form game. In each period, a continuum of buyers and sellers seek to buy and sell houses. Buyers and sellers, however, are unable to transact directly and must instead work through agents, each of whom has limited capacity. Each agent offers a buyer price and a seller price for intermediation services. Buyers and sellers then choose agents, having observed these menus of prices. After buyers and sellers match to agents, each agent decides which other agents he is willing to work with. Facilitating a transaction between a buyer and a seller requires that both the buyer's agent and the seller's agent are willing to work with each other.
In our setting, agents can maintain high prices by refusing to work with any other agent who undercuts either of the “agreed upon” buyer and seller prices. This behavior endogenously lowers the quality of a price deviator, as a price deviator can no longer facilitate transactions between his buyers (sellers) and another agent's sellers (buyers). Because each agent has limited capacity, a buyer (seller) working with a price deviator has access to fewer sellers (buyers). This argument implies that cutting prices by a small amount is not enough to attract buyers and sellers, as they understand that any price deviator will find it much more difficult to facilitate transactions. It is therefore possible to maintain prices above marginal cost while ensuring that a price deviator who does attract buyers and sellers will not profit from his actions.7
Of course, it must be incentive-compatible for agents to refuse to work with a price deviator. We rely here on the repeated interactions among agents. An agent who offers standard prices is willing to forgo working with a price deviator today if he is sufficiently rewarded for doing so in the future. To see why, note first that such an agent's forgone profits are small, since the agent only has a small fraction of the population of buyers and sellers. Second, an agent who offers standard prices is incentivized to follow the prescribed punishment strategy because, if he does not, future play reverts to an equilibrium in which he obtains no profits in all future periods. By contrast, if every agent who offers standard prices punishes a price deviator as prescribed, future play moves to a collusive punishment phase in which every agent other than the price deviator obtains positive rents. Thus, an agent who offers standard prices would be willing to forgo working with a price deviator today for reasonably high discount factors—even as the number of agents becomes arbitrarily large.
One may find it hard to believe that real estate agents would, in fact, engage in such behavior. However, this behavior is well documented. In one particularly egregious example, Birger and Caplin (2004) report that the proprietress of a new discount realty, You Win Realty, was not only boycotted by other realtors in the area but also harassed through threatening phone calls and the like, with one competing realtor threatening to report her to the Federal Trade Commission(!). Taking a broader view, a report by the Department of Justice and Federal Trade Commission (2007) documents that such “steering” behavior (away from discount realtors) is commonly reported, and an earlier investigation by the Federal Trade Commission (1983) finds that 84% of alternative brokers reported frequent or occasional “refusals by other brokers to show homes listed by [their] business,” with 49% saying that this was a common problem.
More recently, the discount Internet-based real estate brokerage Redfin has attempted to disrupt the high fee structure of real estate agency. Redfin has found it difficult, however, to work with traditional real estate agents. In response, Redfin has documented the extreme lengths that real estate agents have gone to so as to avoid working with Redfin agents, going so far as to post a “Hall of Shame” of such agents. Glenn Kelman, CEO of Redfin, explained in his Congressional testimony that “Many agents have told our clients that the sellers would never see their offer because it came from Redfin” (Kelman (2006)).8
Other work, such as that by Levitt and Syverson (2008a), argues that traditional agents as a group would be better off if all such agents would forswear working with discount brokers.9However, unlike our study, they do not explain why an individual traditional broker would give up individually profitable opportunities to work with discount brokers—passing up an opportunity as a traditional seller's agent to work with a discount buyer's agent may cost the traditional agent thousands of dollars. Thus, given the very large number of active real estate agents and firms, Han and Strange (2015) note in their extensive survey of the economics of residential housing markets that it “has puzzled observers […] how a collusive equilibrium, if [it] exists, could be maintained in the industry.” We provide an explanation for this puzzle by explicitly modeling agents' incentives to ostracize (or not ostracize) price deviators. We show that a “traditional” agent can be incentivized to forgo opportunities to work with price-cutting agents in equilibrium.10Moreover, by fully modeling each agent's incentives, we can characterize the set of prices sustainable in equilibrium—in contrast with earlier work, which takes traditional prices as given.11
Our equilibrium is necessarily more complex than one constructed with simple penal codes à la Abreu (1988). In repeated normal-form games, Abreu (1988) demonstrates that simple penal codes are sufficient for implementing maximally collusive strategies. In repeated extensive-form game settings such as ours, by contrast, simple penal codes may not be sufficient for characterizing maximal collusion: As Mailath, Nocke, and White (2017) note, the analysis of repeated extensive-form games may require more involved responses to deviations. The need to reward agents for punishing an initial deviation within-period implies that rewarding agents in future periods may be more important than just punishing the initial deviator as much as possible in future periods.12Accordingly, in our model, we construct an equilibrium that promises sufficient rewards in future periods to each price-conforming agent to incentivize him to punish a price-deviator in-period.
Our analysis not only explains how real estate agents can maintain prices appreciably above cost, but also allows us to assess different proposed policy responses. Han and Hong (2011) investigate “rebate bans,” laws that prohibit buyers' agents from sharing their commissions with buyers, although agents are still allowed to pay buyers' closing costs; 10 states have rebate bans in effect. Why real estate agents lobby for such bans is somewhat curious, as in general buyers' agents do not pay closing costs, in which case such a ban is not binding at the margin—in a standard model of an oligopolistic Bertrand market, a nonbinding rebate ban should not affect equilibrium prices. However, as we show in Theorem3, rebate bans facilitate higher commissions in our setting even though they offer room for real estate agents to reduce buyers' (closing) costs. Thus, Theorem3shows that eliminates such bans would reduce (though not eliminate) the scope for collusion. Our finding on rebate bans is consistent with the view of the Department of Justice (2005) as expressed in their complaint inU.S.A. v. Kentucky Real Estate Commissionand even accords with surprisingly candid remarks of real estate agents themselves.13,14Similarly, our work can explain why real estate agents lobby for “minimum service requirements,” which require brokers to provide a full package of client services; eight states have such a requirement (Bernheim and Meer (2013)).
Barwick (2018) proposed that “agency fees,” that is, the commissions that a seller agent pays to a buyer agent upon the completion of a transaction, should be banned. As Barwick (2018) notes, countries that have banned agency fees have lower overall commissions (though the commissions paid by buyers increase). In our framework, the optimal collusive scheme involves fully exploiting sellers while possibly charging buyers less than cost. Eliminating agency fees would make it infeasible to set a buyer fee that is below cost, since in that case agents would no longer be willing to represent buyers. But prices for sellers would fall to a greater extent than prices for buyers would rise, and so eliminating agency fees would benefit consumers overall, consistent with the argument of Barwick (2018).
The remainder of this paper is organized as follows. SectionIdevelops our model. SectionIIcharacterizes the optimal collusive prices. SectionIIIconsiders the implications of our work for policy—SectionIII.Aconsiders the effects of rebate bans on prices, SectionIII.Bconsiders the effects of eliminating agency fees on prices, and SectionsIII.CandIII.Dconsider alternative ways to structure real estate transactions. SectionIVconcludes.

We require that bothκB$\kappa _B$andκS$\kappa _S$are less than one-half; that is, no agent can represent more than half of the buyers in any given period, and no agent can represent more than half of the sellers in any given period. We also require that(|A|−2)κB≥1$(|A| - 2)\kappa _B\ge 1$and(|A|−2)κS≥1$(|A| - 2)\kappa _S\ge 1$; that is, all buyers and all sellers can be assigned an agent even if two agents are excluded from the economy. Time is discrete and infinite, and agents have a common discount factorδ∈(0,1)$\delta \in (0, 1)$.
We can think of the set of accepted invitations as generating a(directed) network, where an edge froma∼$\tilde{a}$toa$a$represents the fact thata∼$\tilde{a}$has accepteda$a$’s invitation (and soa∼$\tilde{a}$’s buyers have access toa$a$’s sellers). Once the network has formed, we model the housing market as a market in which each buyer has a unique acceptable seller and each seller has a unique acceptable buyer. LetMt$\mathcal {M}_t$be a measurable bijective correspondence betweenBt$B_t$andSt$S_t$unknown to all market participants. The correspondenceMt$\mathcal {M}_t$is a reduced-form way of modeling the preferences of buyers and sellers: For a given buyerb$b$,Mt(b)∈St$\mathcal {M}_t(b) \in S_t$represents the unique seller whose house is desirable for buyerb$b$. A buyerb$b$will consummate a transaction with a sellers$s$if and only ifs$s$’s agent,at(s)${\mathbf{a}}_{t}(s)$, invitedb$b$’s agent,at(b)${\mathbf{a}}_{t}(b)$, andat(b)${\mathbf{a}}_{t}(b)$acceptedat(s)${\mathbf{a}}_{t}(s)$’s invitation, that is, if and only ifat(s)∈Atat(b)⇒${\mathbf{a}}_{t}(s)\in {A}_{t}^{{\mathbf{a}}_{t}(b)\Rightarrow}$. We call the fixed value that a buyer receives from a transaction thebuyer surplusvB$v_{B}$and, similarly, the fixed value that a seller receives from a transaction theseller surplusvS$v_{S}$.
While our model is of course stylized, our assumptions reflect relevant characteristics of the real estate agency market.
First, we note that our assumption that agents charge a fixed fee (as opposed to a percentage of the sale price) is innocuous because buyers and sellers are homogeneous. Indeed, the homogeneity assumption implies that a percentage of the sale price corresponds to a fixed fee; the agents could then calibrate those percentages to obtain the fees calculated in our construction.
Our assumption that there is a unique desirable house for each buyer is designed to capture the idea that buyer preferences are highly heterogeneous. While our assumption is stark, it captures the importance to a buyer of having a large number of houses to consider and to a seller of having a large number of potential buyers.18Adding more realistic preferences for buyers over houses would significantly complicate the model without adding additional insight; the key assumption is that the expected value to a buyer (seller) from hiring a given agent is increasing in the number of sellers (buyers) linked directly or indirectly to that agent.
While we abstract from the bargaining process between a buyer and seller who have been matched by agents, we could instead allow each matched buyer-seller pair to bargain over the surplus generated by a transaction à la Hall and Milgrom (2008). In that case,vB$v_{B}$andvS$v_{S}$would represent the (expected) gains for the buyer and seller, respectively, from that bargaining process. Under the assumption that there is a unique desirable house for each buyer, extending the model in this way would have no effect on our results.
Finally, we impose the orderingκS≤κB$\kappa _S\le \kappa _B$from our understanding that listing a house for sale and the other duties of a selling agent are appreciably more complex and time-consuming than helping a buyer find a suitable house. This ordering is not important for maintaining collusion but does play a role in determining which side of the market pays higher prices, as we discuss after Theorem1in SectionII.
In our setting, perfect collusion can be sustained even in the stage game via coordinated behavior by buyers and sellers. To see this, note that we can support any nonnegative prices in a subgame-perfect Nash equilibrium of the stage game by having buyers and sellers “coordinate” on not signing up with any agent if some agent deviates on prices. Perfect collusion is thus achieved by having both buyers and sellers refuse to sign up with any agent if prices are not as expected. Such a behavior is an equilibrium because it is (weakly) optimal for no seller to sign up with any agent if no buyers sign up with any agent and, similarly, it is (weakly) optimal for no buyer to sign up with any agent if no sellers sign up with any agent.19
However, the coordination failure equilibria just described are unrealistic in our setting, as they involve a very large number of buyers and sellers coordinating among themselves to facilitate collusion by agents. Moreover, a version of the model with a finite number of buyers and sellers who sign up for agents sequentially would not admit such coordination failure equilibria. Also, such equilibria would not be robust to allowing firms to offerinsulating tariffsà la Weyl (2010) and White and Weyl (2016), that is, if brokers can promise to compensate end users if the anticipated number of users on the other side do not show up, coordination failure by end users cannot be used to support collusive equilibria. To avoid such pathological outcomes, we restrict attention to(buyer-and-seller) coordination-proof equilibria, which require that no positive mass of buyers and/or sellers, by altering their actions simultaneously, can strictly improve the expected welfare of all of them. Formally, a subgame-perfect Nash equilibrium is(buyer-and-seller) coordination-proofif, fixing the strategy profile of the agents, for every periodt$t$and every history of play up tot$t$, there does not exist a positive-measure subsetB¯$\bar{B}$of buyers and/or positive-measure subsetS¯$\bar{S}$of sellers that, in the agent selection phase, can jointly submit different ordered lists that result in higher expected utility for each market participant inB¯∪S¯$\bar{B} \cup \bar{S}$. Our equilibrium restriction prevents miscoordination among buyers and sellers as a mechanism to support higher prices. Our refinement here is in the spirit of coalition-proofness à la Bernheim, Peleg, and Whinston (1987) among the short-lived buyers and sellers.20Alternatively, one could use a suitably adapted version of the coalitional rationalizability concept introduced by Ambrus (2006) to avoid this type of miscoordination among short-lived buyers and sellers.

Buyers are offered better prices than sellers in the highest sustainable-profit equilibrium, and those prices may in fact be negative. However, the reason for subsidizing buyers is not related to the idea from platform economics that it can be optimal to subsidize one side of the market to encourage adoption by the other side of the market. Under the strategy profile we have specified, in each period each agent will offer prices(pB★,pS★)$(p^{\star }_B, p^{\star }_S)$, buyers and sellers will list agents arbitrarily, and the full network forms with standard fees. We now verify that the strategy profile constitutes a coordination-proof subgame-perfect Nash equilibrium.
Case 1—Collusive Pricing: It is straightforward that no positive masses of buyers and sellers can alter their actions to simultaneously improve their welfare since every agent offers a buyer pricepB★≤vB$p^{\star }_B\le v_{B}$to buyers, every agent offers a seller pricepS★≤vS$p^{\star }_S\le v_{S}$to sellers, every buyer and seller obtains an agent, and the full network forms (regardless of buyer and seller actions).

Intuitively, buyers and sellers demand a nonnegligible discount to work witha˚$\mathring{a}$because, by lowering his prices,a˚$\mathring{a}$has effectively become a lower quality agent because other agents no longer work witha˚$\mathring{a}$. Hence, the ability of other agents to coordinate on ostracizinga˚$\mathring{a}$generates a natural discreteness in the set of locally optimal prices. This is in contrast to typical models of Bertrand competition in which any price reduction is enough to attract consumers.
Moreover, the discount demanded by buyers is different than the discount demanded by sellers: BecauseκS$\kappa _S$is smaller thanκB$\kappa _B$, each buyer working with the price deviator has a lower probability of consummating a transaction than each seller working with the price deviator, as each buyer has access to onlyκS$\kappa _S$sellers whereas each seller has access toκB$\kappa _B$buyers. Thus, a small decrease inpB★$p^{\star }_B$has a larger effect onpB∘$p^{\circ }_B$than the same small decrease inpS★$p^{\star }_S$has onpS∘$p^{\circ }_S$. This effect drives the price asymmetry of Theorem1—cutting prices for buyers is more efficient for forestalling price deviations than cutting prices for sellers.31
We next verify that the prescribed strategy profile is incentive-compatible with respect to making and accepting invitations in the cooperation phase. It is straightforward to show that an agent cannot profitably deviate with respect to making and accepting invitations in the collusive pricing and ineffective price deviation cases. It is also straightforward to show that, after an effective price deviation bya˚$\mathring{a}$, an agent other thana˚$\mathring{a}$cannot profitably deviate with respect to making and accepting invitations from agents other thana˚$\mathring{a}$and thata˚$\mathring{a}$’s prescribed actions are optimal. Details are given in AppendixC.A.
It is immediate thata˚$\mathring{a}$should not offer more thanpSa˚$p^{\mathring{a}}_S$as part of an invitation to any other agenta$a$. First,a˚$\mathring{a}$cannot benefit in-period from such an offer, since he would take a loss from any transactions witha$a$if that offer were accepted. Second, there is no future benefit to such an offer, sincea˚$\mathring{a}$will receive zero future profits regardless. Similarly,a˚$\mathring{a}$will never accept any invitation with a transfer fee of less than−pBa˚$-p^{\mathring{a}}_B$.
Intuitively, each agenta$a$other thana˚$\mathring{a}$is unwilling to work with the price deviator since the number of buyers and sellers represented bya$a$this period is roughly proportional toα$\alpha$and future profits fora$a$are also roughly proportional toα$\alpha$. Thus, for reasonably high discount factors, future profits fora$a$are worth more than the gains from working witha˚$\mathring{a}$today.
The analysis of making and responding to invitations in thea˚$\mathring{a}$-collusive and{a˚,a}$\lbrace \mathring{a}, a\rbrace$-collusive punishment phases proceeds very similarly to the analysis of making and responding to invitations in the cooperation phase. The one additional consideration is that we require nonpunished agents to not work with punished agents. However, since buyers and sellers do not work with any punished agent, it is easy to incentivize other agents to not work with the punished agent(s).35Details are given in AppendixC.A.
The equilibrium described in Theorem1obtains the highest profits of any coordination-proof subgame-perfect Nash equilibrium, but it requires that real estate agents coordinate on a specific price change after any price deviation. Such coordination may be infeasible in a collusive scheme involving a large number of independent agents. In this section, we build on our prior analysis to demonstrate that there exists anexclusion equilibriumin which prices never change, and so collusion is enforced solely through the exclusion of any deviators. We further demonstrate that when the number of agents is large—which reflects the institutional reality of the real estate agency industry—the highest-price exclusion equilibrium achieves the same prices as those in Theorem1.
Anexclusion equilibriumis a (coordination-proof) subgame-perfect Nash equilibrium in which the same prices are prescribed after every history, that is, prices dictated by the equilibrium are the same in every period regardless of previous play.
Theorem 2.Forδ≥12$\delta \ge \frac{1}{2}$, there exists an exclusion equilibrium with prices37


Corollary 1.As market concentration approaches zero, the prices of any highest-profit (coordination-proof) subgame-perfect Nash equilibrium (i.e., the prices of Theorem1) and the prices of any highest profit exclusion equilibrium converge to(qB★,qS★)$(q^{\star }_B, q^{\star }_S)$.
Corollary1demonstrates that when the number of agents is large, little in the way of industry profits is sacrificed by using a simple and intuitive exclusion equilibrium. In particular, as depicted in Figure3, as market concentration goes to zero, the prices charged in the equilibria supporting Theorems1and2become arbitrarily close, that is,limα→0(pB★,pS★)=(qB★,qS★)$\lim _{\alpha \rightarrow 0} (p^{\star }_B, p^{\star }_S) = (q^{\star }_B, q^{\star }_S)$. Thus, in practice, we would expect real estate agents to simply use one set of prices and maintain collusion purely through excluding deviators. This comports with the examples and evidence about real estate agent behavior described in the introduction.
There exist exclusion equilibria that support the prices of Theorem2and that are particularly simple to implement. InInternet AppendixSectionIII, we construct an exclusion equilibrium supporting(qB★,qS★)$(q^{\star }_B, q^{\star }_S)$in which an agent is punished only if either he both cuts prices and recruits other agents to work with him, or he works with an agent who has cut prices.38Thus, collusion can be an equilibrium outcome even when it is difficult for agents to monitor other agents' internal transactions (i.e., transactions in which an agent represents both the buyer and the seller).
From the prices given in Theorem1, we can see that, as would occur in standard models of collusion, the highest sustainable price declines as market concentration decreases, that is,∂pB★∂α>0$\frac{\partial p^{\star }_B}{ \partial \alpha } > 0$. The reason for this comparative static can be seen from inequality (14)—as industry concentration falls, the value of maintaining collusion falls while the benefit of an effective price deviation does not change. Intuitively, lower industry concentration gives each agent lower profits, and thus we must reduce the equilibrium price to prevent each agent from having an incentive to deviate. In contrast to standard models, however, prices do not approach competitive levels as the industry concentration goes to zero—because an effective price deviation requires a sufficiently large price cut,(pB★,pS★)$(p^{\star }_B, p^{\star }_S)$does not have to drop all the way to competitive pricing to deter such a deviation.
Our results show that prices appreciably above marginal cost can be sustained regardless of the number of agents. This is in sharp contrast to classical models of collusion, in which the highest sustainable price falls to marginal cost as the number of firms becomes large (i.e.,α→0$\alpha \rightarrow 0$). Under classical “grim trigger” strategies, the punishment for a deviating firm is always zero, the benefits from cooperation go to zero as the number of firms becomes large, and the gains for a deviating firm are constant and positive, and thus collusion is impossible to sustain below a sufficiently low market concentration. By contrast, while in our equilibrium the punishment for a deviating firm is always zero and the benefits from cooperation go to zero as the number of firms becomes large, at the same time the gains for a deviating firm also go to 0 as the number of firms becomes large because agents exclude a price deviator in-period.
Formally, to compare our results with the standard analysis of Bertrand competition games, we consider here the simpler grim trigger strategies that also allow agents to extract full surplus. The grim trigger strategy profile that obtains full-surplus extraction involves every agent offering a buyer price ofvB$v_{B}$and a seller price ofvS$v_{S}$, statically optimal network formation (regardless of offered prices), and any deviation leading to Bertrand competition in future periods. If agents are playing a grim trigger strategy profile, then an optimal deviation involves anε$\epsilon$price cut to buyers and sellers and statically optimal network formation actions; this generates a profit of(vB+vS)κS$(v_{B}+ v_{S})\kappa _S$for the deviating agent. Adhering to a grim trigger strategy generates a profit ofα1−δ(vB+vS)$\frac{\alpha }{1-\delta }(v_{B}+ v_{S})$.
It follows that grim trigger strategies will only be effective if the market concentrationα$\alpha$is higher than(1−δ)κS$(1 - \delta)\kappa _S$, and hence market concentration must be quite high to maintain collusion using grim trigger strategies. Moreover, grim trigger strategies cannot facilitate any collusion when market concentration is below(1−δ)κS$(1 - \delta)\kappa _S$—that is, whenα<(1−δ)κS$\alpha < (1 - \delta)\kappa _S$, grim trigger strategies cannot support any prices above marginal cost. By contrast, the strategy profile described in SectionII.Aallows full-surplus extraction by the agents so long asα≥(1−δ)κBκS$\alpha \ge (1 - \delta)\kappa _B\kappa _S$(which is less than(1−δ)κS$(1 - \delta)\kappa _S$). Moreover, whenα<(1−δ)κBκS$\alpha < (1 - \delta)\kappa _B\kappa _S$, our strategy profile continues to facilitate significant surplus extraction, that is, agents can effectively collude even as the number of agents grows large, in which case we might naïvely expect the market to become “perfectly competitive.” In particular, even asα→0$\alpha \rightarrow 0$, the strategy profile that we construct generates strictly positive surplus for the industry since an agent who deviates on price finds himself excluded from the network and thus unable to make a profit.
Two characteristics of the model are crucial for the existence of highly collusive behavior even when the number of players is large. First, both buyers and sellers obtain independent representation but expect their representative to work with the representatives of others; this structure allows for exclusion-based in-period punishments of the type we describe.39Adding a restriction that buyers and sellers can meet only when they use the same agent switches the model to a traditional platform model with limited capacity; under this alternative structure, collusion can no longer be maintained when the number of agents is large. Such a change, however, reduces total welfare and can even lower consumer surplus since preventing network formation leads to an inefficient lack of transactions in the market—each buyer has at mostκS$\kappa _S$probability of buying a house, and each seller has at mostκB$\kappa _B$probability of selling a house. IfκB$\kappa _B$andκS$\kappa _S$are small, few transactions will be completed, reducing the gains from competitive pricing.
Second, the effect ofκS$\kappa _S$andκB$\kappa _B$on the highest sustainable price highlights the other feature of our model that allows for high prices in an unconcentrated industry: Matching buyers and sellers is challenging since there is only one potential buyer for each house and only one acceptable house for each buyer. While extreme, these assumptions capture the importance of match quality in the housing market. If, by contrast, each house were acceptable to a large number of buyers and each buyer found many houses acceptable, collusion would be much more difficult to sustain. In that case, an entrant offering service to both sides of the market would need only offer slightly lower prices to attract both buyers and sellers, as a buyer (or seller) would still have a high probability of finding an acceptable match even if the entrant were excluded from the network. Thus, when buyers and sellers have a large number of possible transaction partners, the price for facilitating a transaction should be close to marginal cost.
Examples of intermediated markets that do not appear to be collusive include ridesharing firms and travel agents selling airline tickets. However, in both of these markets, intermediaries have sufficient capacity to serve all of one side of the market—ridesharing firms have effectively infinite capacity on both sides of the market, while a typical travel agent effectively represents all airlines. In fact, in both the ridesharing and travel agency markets, sellers “multi-home” and typically work with each platform intermediary, and thus there is no need for platform intermediaries to form a network, and so our analysis does not apply. Even if ridesharing firms exhibited limited capacity on both sides of the market, the fact that match quality does not matter very much would imply that the ability to punish other firms via network exclusion would be very weak. Thus, even in this case, we would not expect firms to be able to sustain high prices.
A key requirement for successful collusion in our setting is that agents are able to observe other agents' pricing on both the buy and sell sides, that is, we rule out “secret” price undercutting (i.e., price undercutting that is observable by customers but is not observable by other agents). In this type of market, it would be difficult for a real estate agent to inform potential customers of lower prices through advertising or similar other means without also alerting other real estate agents. Of course, in isolated cases, real estate agents could (and from anecdotes we have heard occasionally do) offer secret price cuts or rebates. But these cuts are not widely advertised, which is inconsistent with an effort to draw in a significant amount of new business through such undercutting. Rather, the idiosyncratic nature of these deals and the effort expended to keep such deals secret instead supports our argument that open price cuts would lead to retaliatory behavior by other agents.
Furthermore, we do not need prices to be perfectly observable to facilitate collusion. Even if the probability that other agents will not detect a price deviation is small, there continue to exist equilibria in which the prices charged to buyers and sellers are bounded away from marginal costs even as the number of agents becomes arbitrarily large. One form of these equilibria requires designating a fixed number of agents as “insiders” who each obtain a small but nonvanishing share of surplus each period, while “outsiders” are largely excluded from the market. Insiders do not cut prices since the loss of future profits is greater than the expected gains from cutting prices today, and prices are chosen (as in the collusive punishment phase of our main equilibrium) so that no outsider can attract buyers and sellers while still making a positive profit.
The dynamics that we identify here can help explain why entry, including entry by discount realtors and the like, has been unable to drive prices down to competitive levels, which is particularly surprising given that the price of many other financial transactions has collapsed in recent decades—for example, stock commissions and mutual fund fees. Our analysis suggests that, in equilibrium, such low-price entrants can be “frozen out” by market incumbents who refuse to work with them, much in the same way that a price deviator can be ostracized by other agents. Likewise, our analysis can help explain why two-sided real estate platforms (e.g., Redfin) have not been able to break the grip of traditional realtors.40,41Agents can exclude a discount platform similarly to how they can exclude a deviating agent. To get around being excluded, the platform entrant must, at a minimum, be quite large (in the sense of having high capacity to serve both home buyers and home sellers), since otherwise it will not prove valuable to customers as it will lack access to home buyers and sellers not on the platform. But most entrants cannot achieve high capacity at launch, especially in a two-sided market such as real estate in which a firm must provide services to both buyers and sellers. Moreover, achieving high capacity in real estate brokerage services is particularly difficult as expanding capacity means hiring new employees, and those employees must possess real estate licenses as well as considerable implicit local knowledge.
article.III. Policy Responses:
One institutional feature of real estate brokerage is that the buyer pricepB★$p^{\star }_B$is usually zero, with the buyer's agent receiving his compensation via the agency fee set by the seller. In practice, this convention means that any competition for buyer representation must be in the form of “rebates” from buyers' agents to buyers, under which part of the agency fee paid by the seller's agent to the buyer's agent is then passed on to the buyer. The presence of rebates therefore gives agents representing buyers the ability to set the price for buyers. In the context of real estate regulation, a “rebate ban” prohibits a buyer's agent from paying a buyer for the right to represent him in a real estate transaction, that is, prohibits a buyer's agent from rebating part of the commission that he receives from the seller's agent back to the buyer. Such rebate bans are a feature of real estate regulation in several U.S. states (Magura (2007)). Han and Hong (2011) argue that rebate bans are anticompetitive and estimate a model in which rebate bans generate excessive entry into real estate brokerage.
In our framework, we confirm that rebate bans can play an important role in increasing the profitability of collusive behavior by agents. We model arebate banas a constraint whereby each agent must offer a weakly positive buyer price, that is,pB,ta≥0$p^a_{B,t} \ge 0$for all agentsa∈A$a \in A$and all timest$t$. Rebate bans can facilitate collusion even when no agent is offering a zero price to buyers, as rebate bans constrain off-equilibrium-path pricing—for a given collusive pricing scheme, a deviator may need to charge a negative price to attract buyers, as this is the only way to sufficiently compensate buyers for their reduced access to sellers. Thus, when negative buyer prices are prohibited, it is harder for the deviator to recruit buyers and hence higher prices for buyers (and higher profits) can be sustained.

Figure4plots the prices offered to buyers and sellers in the highest sustainable-profit equilibrium as a function of market concentration when a rebate ban is present. Intuitively, rebate bans have an effect only when the optimal deviating buyer pricepB∘$p^{\circ }_B$is negative; this happens when market concentration is sufficiently low, that is,α̂<κSvSvS+(1−κS)vB$\hat{\alpha }< \kappa _S\frac{v_{S}}{v_{S}+ (1-\kappa _S)v_{B}}$. Thus, when market concentration is sufficiently high,α̂≥κSvSvS+(1−κS)vB$\hat{\alpha }\ge \kappa _S\frac{v_{S}}{v_{S}+ (1-\kappa _S)v_{B}}$, the rebate ban has no effect. By contrast, when market concentration is sufficiently low,α̂<κSvSvS+(1−κS)vB$\hat{\alpha }< \kappa _S\frac{v_{S}}{v_{S}+ (1-\kappa _S)v_{B}}$, the collusive buyer pricepB★$p^{\star }_B$is set so that buyers would find working with an agent with a buyer price of zero (and getting access only to that agent's sellers) to be weakly dispreferred to working with an agent with a buyer price ofpB★$p^{\star }_B$(and getting access to all sellers).
In our baseline model, all agents are ex ante identical, and we show that a symmetric equilibrium delivers the highest sustainable surplus extraction. Within the real estate industry, however, there is at least some distinction between agents who primarily serve buyers and agents who primarily serve sellers. In particular, while any agent can and likely does represent buyers at times, there are agents, and even firms of agents, that exclusively represent buyers. Indeed, there exists a trade association for such agents, the National Association of Exclusive Buyer Agents.
To capture the potential for different roles among agents, we generalize our model to allow for two types of agents.Seller-proficient agentshave a low cost for acting as a seller's agent and a potentially high cost for acting as a buyer's agent.Buyer-exclusive agentshave a low cost for acting as a buyer's agent and a prohibitively high cost for acting as a seller's agent. As we show, agent specialization can play an important role in the structure of optimal collusion, and that role can depend on the presence of agency fees. In particular, buyer-exclusive agents cannot be induced to set negative buyer prices unless they are compensated through agency fees, that is, the fee that the seller agent pays to the buyer agent upon a successful transaction.
In the United States, the price paid by a buyer to his agent is typically zero, despite some costs for the agent of representing a buyer; this is consistent with our model, which suggests that optimal collusion might require agents to subsidize buyers. Thus, considering agent specialization allows us to investigate the effects of eliminating agency fees (“decoupling”), a reform that Barwick (2018) calls for. This policy proposal has gained prominence in the wake of the verdict inSitzer v. NAR et al.that industry rules regarding agency fees were anticompetitive. Indeed, decoupling has been proposed as an injunctive remedy in the case (Rodriguez (2023)). Moreover, asSitzerwas being adjudicated, both government leaders (Biden (2021)) and scholars (Nadel (2021), Barry, Fried, and Hatfield (2023)) argued for curtailing anticompetitive behavior in real estate agency by eliminating agency fees, arguing that such fees were at the heart of the anticompetitive conduct alleged in the case. More recently, the Department of Justice (2024) has called for decoupling in its statement of interest regarding theNosalekcase.43Our results strengthen the case for decoupling: Corollary2shows that the highest sustainable-profit equilibrium may well have lower prices if agency fees are eliminated.44
We now characterize the highest profits that can be collectively achieved by the industry.

It is immediate from the statement of the theorem that prices are higher under specialization. Prices are higher because a two-sided deviation is less profitable for seller-proficient agents (and impossible for buyer-exclusive agents); meanwhile, no agent incurs the additional costc$c$along the equilibrium path, as each agent only works with one side of the market. In fact, if working with buyers is sufficiently costly for seller-proficient agents (i.e.,c≥vB+vS$c\ge v_{B}+ v_{S}$), monopoly prices can be sustained. Equilibrium prices are depicted in Figure5. Note that when market concentration is low enough, buyers are charged negative prices.
Whereas agent specialization increases the scope for collusion, eliminating agency fees may reduce it. In the context of our model, eliminating agency fees requires that, if the seller-proficient agenta$a$invitesa∼∈AB∪AS$\tilde{a}\in A_B \cup A_S$, he offers a fee offta∼←a=0$f^{\tilde{a} \leftarrow a}_t = 0$for all agentsa$a$anda∼$\tilde{a}$and each timet$t$; we call this thezero-fees model. In response to eliminating agency fees, there are two candidate equilibria to maximize industry profits. In the first, seller-proficient agents represent both buyers and sellers. In the second, buyer-exclusive agents represent buyers while seller-proficient agents represent sellers. The advantage of the first equilibrium, in which seller-proficient agents represent buyers, is that buyers can still be charged negative prices as seller-proficient agents' profits from representing sellers will more than recover the costs of representing buyers. Note that buyer-exclusive agents cannot profitably undercut in this first equilibrium, as it is easy to incentivize seller-proficient agents to ostracize them.

Moreover, industry revenue is weakly less than the highest sustainable profits when agency fees are allowed. However,limσ→0(pB★+pS★)=(vB+vS)(1−κS)+κSc$\lim _{\sigma \rightarrow 0} (p^{\star }_B+ p^{\star }_S) = (v_B + v_S)(1 - \kappa _S) + \kappa _Sc$, which is the same revenue in the limit as under the equilibrium that supports the highest sustainable profits when agency fees are allowed.

The equilibrium supporting the prices stated in Theorem5is similar to the equilibrium supporting the prices when agent specialization is not present, that is, the equilibrium described after Theorem1, except that agency fees are now set to zero. However, in the equilibrium described by Theorem5, there are two additional incentive constraints. First, we need to ensure that buyer-exclusive agents cannot profit by offering buyers a better price; we do this by requiring seller-proficient agents not to work with a buyer-exclusive agent even if that agent attracts buyers. Second, we may need to incentivize seller-proficient agents to represent buyers each period through dynamic considerations, because whenpB★$p^{\star }_B$is less thanc$c$(i.e., the price charged to buyers does not compensate an agent for the cost of representing that buyer), a seller-proficient agent would be better off not representing buyers. Neither of these additional incentive constraints binds so long asδ≥12$\delta \ge \frac{1}{2}$. The full proof of Theorem5is relegated toInternet AppendixSectionV.
As Figure6demonstrates, the equilibrium price is lower under the equilibrium of Theorem5than under the equilibrium of Theorem4(in which buyer-exclusive agents represent buyers and are compensated through agency fees). The equilibrium price for buyers is lower as individual equilibrium profits are lower (since seller-proficient agents now inefficiently represent buyers). But individual equilibrium profits go to zero as the market becomes highly unconcentrated regardless of whether agency fees are allowed. Thus, as the market becomes highly unconcentrated, the highest sustainable buyer price when agency fees are allowed (given in Theorem4) and the highest sustainable buyer price when buyers work with seller-proficient agents (given in Theorem5) converge.

The interesting case of Theorem6is when buyer valuations are sufficiently low. In that case, buyers are charged a zero price and sellers are charged a price less thanvS$v_{S}$. This is in contrast to the prices charged to maximize industry profits when agency fees are allowed (Theorem4) or when seller-proficient agents represent buyers (Theorem5). This hard floor for buyer prices limits the set of sustainable seller prices: For any seller price higher thanpS★$p^{\star }_S$, a seller-proficient agent could choose prices sufficiently low to attract buyers and sellers while still increasing profits. Thus, eliminating agency fees may appreciably decrease the scope for collusion, as suggested by Barwick (2018).47We formalize this statement in Corollary2.

Our model can also explain why a number of proposed responses for private actors have failed to gain traction. One especially simple proposed solution is to simply encourage more “for-sale-by-owner” properties. In terms of our model, we can consider a seller representing himself as a one-period agent who has very small sell-side capacity and no buy-side capacity and who deviates on price; an equilibrium in which agents ostracize such self-representing sellers (in a manner similar to how agents ostracize price-deviating agents) can be constructed in a manner analogous to our analysis here. Indeed, consistent with this prediction, for-sale-by-owner homes take much longer to sell than sales via agents (Hendel, Nevo, and Ortalo-Magné (2009)). Moreover, since most sellers are simultaneously buying a house (and are unwilling or unable to pay two mortgages simultaneously), such delay is costly. Consistent with the foregoing, arm's-length sales of for-sale-by-owner homes make up only 5% of the market and are in decline (hitting an all-time low in 2017) (National Association of Realtors (2017)).
Similarly, some have proposed that buyers represent themselves, arguing that a greater willingness of buyers to self-represent would induce agents to compete to attract buyers. We do not think this is a solution for two reasons. First, our model suggests that this would not work. We may think of a self-representing buyer as a one-period agent who has a very small buy-side capacity and no sell-side capacity and who deviates on price. Like the case of seller self-representation, if we allowed buyers to represent themselves in our model, real estate agents could still easily enforce the collusive commission rate by either refusing to work with buyers who self-represent or demanding the entire commission in transactions with those buyers. Consistent with this prediction, buyers rarely represent themselves in typical real estate transactions in practice (National Association of Realtors (2020)). Additionally, in many states, buyer self-representation is not really an option—the advantages for a buyer of representing himself are closed off entirely, as it is illegal for nonlicensed agents to collect a commission (even when representing themselves).48
Another recent innovation in the real estate market is the rise of iBuyers, who make all-cash offers to homeowners. A homeowner could sell directly to such an iBuyer instead of using a traditional real estate agent. We may think of iBuyers as sell-side agents in our model (with perhaps somewhat larger capacities). Like the other two-sided platforms discussed in SectionII.C.5, our model predicts that iBuyers would charge the same fees to sellers and offer the same transfers to buyers' agents to avoid being ostracized by traditional real estate agents. Indeed, iBuyers typically charge a service fee that is higher than the traditional6%$6\%$(Riquier (2019)).49Furthermore, iBuyers offer the typical agency fee to agents representing buyers who purchase iBuyer-owned homes.50
article.IV. Conclusion:
In this paper, we explain how an extremely unconcentrated brokered industry such as real estate agency can still support collusive pricing.51The brokerage feature is key: Our analysis relies on the fact that, after prices have been announced, brokers must work with each other to complete transactions. Brokers can punish a price deviator within-period by refusing to work with him; this both directly harms the price deviator and makes the price deviator's services less appealing to buyers and sellers. As a result, even though the market has low barriers to entry (and thus many brokers), brokers are able to extract a large fraction of the total surplus.
Our results suggest that there is substantial potential for intermediaries in residential real estate to extract rents; this rent extraction takes the form of a high price for brokering transactions. Given that residential real estate is a key component of the U.S. economy, with approximately one and a half trillion dollars in transactions per year (National Association of Realtors (2018)), if housing demand is at least somewhat elastic, the allocative distortions resulting from broker rents could be quite economically significant.52
By formally modeling brokered intermediation, we can also clarify the effects of proposed and existing policies. Our model indicates that eliminating rebate bans, while not a panacea, can reduce the scope for collusion. Similarly, eliminating agency fees can weaken the ability of industry participants to maintain high prices.
Finally, our work highlights the importance of using repeated extensive-form games to model competition in settings with multistage interactions among market participants.53The strategies analyzed here do not fit the traditional normal-form repeated game analysis paradigm, which may help explain why many economists consider the high commissions of real estate agents puzzling (Hsieh and Moretti (2003)). Moreover, multistage interactions are quite common in intermediated markets, including venture capital, initial public offerings, and syndicated lending (Hochberg, Ljungqvist, and Lu (2010), Hatfield et al. (2020), Cai et al. (2020)). These techniques may also prove useful in analyzing business-to-business transactions and market entry.
